did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the YOLOv7 (You Only Look Once) deep learning algorithm, a convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, to classify skin cancer lesions (melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and basal cell carcinoma) using transfer learning on a dataset of 2792 images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work using the YOLOv7 algorithm to classify skin cancer lesions. It includes a methodology section detailing data collection (2792 images), training, validation, and testing procedures, as well as a results section reporting specific performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) and comparing them with prior literature. Although it contains a literature review section, its primary purpose is to report new technical results from a specific study.

title: Computer-Aided Classification of Skin Cancer based on the YOLO Algorithm
journal: 2024 13th International Conference on Modern Circuits and Systems Technologies (MOCAST)
brief_summary: This study implements the YOLOv7 deep learning algorithm to classify three prevalent skin cancer types: melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and basal cell carcinoma. The model was trained on a dataset of 2,792 non-dermoscopic images collected from sources such as MED-NODE, Google, Bing, and Dermznet NZ, utilizing data augmentation techniques. Evaluation metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity were calculated to assess performance, with the model achieving average values of 89.65%, 85.00%, and 91.90%, respectively. The results indicate superior performance for melanoma classification but insufficient sensitivity for basal cell carcinoma. The authors conclude that while YOLOv7 performs well compared to prior literature, increasing the training dataset size could further improve the model's discriminative capability.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three most prevalent forms of skin cancer studied. Classified using YOLOv7 on 2792 non-dermoscopic images. Average accuracy 85.5%, sensitivity 70.0%, specificity 93.3%.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer (melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and basal cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper implements YOLOv7 for the accurate classification of three prevalent forms of skin lesions: melanoma (MEL), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and basal cell carcinoma (BCC). The model predicts discrete class labels for these cancer types using a dataset of 2792 non-dermoscopic images. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics for each class.

datasets_used.name: Custom Aggregated Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of non-dermoscopic skin cancer images collected from multiple public online sources: the MED-NODE dataset (Department of Dermatology of the Groningen University Medical Center), Google.com, Bing.com, and the Dermznet NZ repository.

use.training: Used for training the YOLOv7 model after data augmentation (rotation, zoom, width shift, shear, brightness change, flipping).
use.validation: Used for validation during the YOLOv7 training process.
use.testing: Used for testing the final model performance.
use.other: MED-NODE subset (70 melanoma, 100 nevus images) was a source component but not used as a standalone dataset in the final split; other sources (Google, Bing, Dermznet NZ) contributed to the aggregate.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) and compared qualitatively with prior literature.

Custom Aggregated Dataset (Non-dermoscop.notes: The paper reports that YOLOv7 performed better for MEL classification. For BCC, it achieved good specificity and accuracy but insufficient sensitivity. For SCC, it outperformed BCC in accuracy and sensitivity but under-performed in specificity. The authors note that increasing the training dataset size could improve performance.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning strategy employed; specific pre-trained weights not explicitly named in the provided text, though the method is described as transfer learning.

architecture_details.hardware_used: Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-7700 CPU (3.60 GHz), NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti, 32 GB RAM

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: rotation, zoom, width shift, shear, brightness change, horizontal flipping, and vertical flipping

conclusions: The YOLOv7 algorithm, implemented via transfer learning on a dataset of 2,792 non-dermoscopic images, achieved average performance metrics of 89.65% accuracy, 85.00% sensitivity, and 91.90% specificity for classifying melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and basal cell carcinoma.
The model demonstrated superior performance for melanoma classification, with high accuracy (95.0%) and sensitivity (98.3%).
The model showed insufficient sensitivity for basal cell carcinoma (70.0%), despite achieving good specificity (93.3%) and accuracy (85.5%).
For squamous cell carcinoma, the model outperformed basal cell carcinoma in accuracy (88.4% vs 85.5%) and sensitivity (86.7% vs 70.0%) but under-performed in specificity (89.4% vs 93.3%).
The proposed YOLOv7 model performed better than several previously published works in the literature, despite using a limited amount of training data.

The authors acknowledge that increasing the size of the training dataset is necessary to significantly boost the model's discriminative capability, as skin lesion images exhibit large variations in features, textures, colors, and shapes.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, non-dermoscopic dataset of only 2,792 images collected from heterogeneous online sources (MED-NODE, Google, Bing, Dermznet NZ), which limits generalizability and introduces potential population and image quality bias compared to standardized dermoscopic datasets like ISIC.
The model demonstrates insufficient sensitivity for Basal Cell Carcinoma (70.0%), which is a critical clinical limitation as missing BCC cases could delay necessary treatment.
The paper acknowledges that increasing the training dataset size is necessary to improve discriminative capability due to the high variability in lesion features, textures, colors, and shapes.

Performance comparisons with prior literature are qualitative and potentially unfair, as the authors note that true impartial comparison requires identical circumstances (dataset, categories, hardware), which were not matched across studies.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; results are presented as descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity).
The dataset split includes a very small validation set (45 images total, 15 per class) and testing set (180 images total, 60 per class), which may lead to high variance in performance estimates.

The study focuses on non-dermoscopic (clinical/digital) images, which are generally more challenging to classify accurately than dermoscopic images, yet claims superior performance to some dermoscopic-based studies without direct head-to-head validation on the same data.
